---
title: "FW 536 — Day 5 morning lab (v3 · anadromous fish & freshwater) — Your Name"
author: "Your Name Here"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    self_contained: true
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: show
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

# Day 5 morning lab — Bayesian modeling problem set 2

**Problem set version:** **v3 — anadromous fish & freshwater theme**.
(The companion version is [v2 — forest carnivore](problem_set_v2.html); you choose whichever theme fits your interests.)

This template matches [`problem_set_v3.html`](problem_set_v3.html). Read each problem statement there, write your work below, **Knit** to HTML, and upload the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.

For setup help see the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html).

---

# Setup

```{r}
# Load packages you'll need. Add or remove as appropriate.
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(nimble)
library(MCMCvis)
library(coda)
```

---

# Problem 1 — Steelhead redd counts across 15 tributaries

> Total redds counted per tributary in a single survey year are modeled as:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 2 — Sockeye spawner-recruit, harvest at 95% confidence

> You have 25 years of $(S_t, R_t)$ for a Bristol Bay sockeye stock. Fit Beverton-Holt and report $S_\text{msy}$, $H_\text{msy}$ posteriors. Then compute the 5th percentile harvest rate (the "precautionary" $H_\text{msy}$).

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 3 — Sockeye $a$ prior: does it change Smsy?

> Re-fit Problem 2 with two different priors on $a$:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 4 — Bull trout occupancy across 8 basins

> 320 stream reaches in 8 basins, presence/absence data plus temperature (scaled). Model:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 5 — Chinook smolt counts at screw traps

> 15 screw-trap sites, each operated nightly for 7 nights. Each night's count $y[i,j]$ is a binomial draw from latent nightly abundance $N_i$ (assumed constant within the week) with capture probability $p$.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 6 — Coho salmon recolonization after dam removal

> 45 reaches above a removed dam, surveyed for 5 years post-removal (3 replicate snorkels per year). You expect colonization $\gamma_t$ to rise as fish access new habitat. Persistence $\phi$ is high (coho return reliably once established).

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 7 — PIT-tag survival of steelhead smolts across years

> 10 release years; in each year, 500 smolts are tagged and survival to adult return is observed. Tag-recovery data summarised as $r_y / R_y$ (recoveries over releases). Fit a binomial model with annual random effects on survival:

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 8 — Egg-to-fry survival from staged binomial

> You released 5,000 eggs in each of 20 redds; in fall, you sampled emergence traps and observed $f_j$ fry per redd. Survival per egg is the latent quantity; model fry as binomial with hierarchical survival.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 9 — Does the basic redd Poisson fit?

> Take Problem 1's redd model and add posterior-predictive Bayesian p-value checks. Compare $T(y) = \text{var}(y)/\text{mean}(y)$ between data and posterior replicates — if the data are overdispersed, this ratio will exceed 1 (Poisson predicts 1).

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# Problem 10 — Probability of quasi-extinction in 20 years

> Combining the spawner-recruit fit from Problem 2 and the hierarchical SAR fit from Problem 7, propagate posterior uncertainty through a 20-year forward population projection. Report $P(N_{t=20} < 100)$ assuming current spawners $S_0 = 500$.

**My work:**

```{r}
# Your R code here
```

**Answer / biological interpretation:**


---

# (Optional) Embed a photo of hand-written work

For problems easier to do on paper, photograph and embed:

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE, out.width="60%", fig.cap="Hand-derivation for Problem N"}
knitr::include_graphics("images/problemN_derivation.jpg")
```

(Change `eval=FALSE` to `eval=TRUE` after saving an image. See the [R Markdown tutorial](../rmarkdown_tutorial.html) for details.)

---

# Submission checklist

- [ ] Answered every morning problem from `problem_set_v3.html`.
- [ ] Knitted to HTML without errors.
- [ ] Photos (if any) embedded with `self_contained: true` in the YAML.
- [ ] Uploaded the `.html` to the matching Canvas assignment.
